A Statistical Analysis of Coughing Patterns on ‘ Who Wants To Be A Millionaire?’
  This is a Medium post about using statistics to understand a process of exponential growth linked to coughing.
  I’m talking about Charles Ingram’s infamous 2001 appearance on  Who Wants To Be A Millionaire? where he (allegedly) cheated his way to win £1 million with the help of his wife, Diana Ingram, and a man called Tecwen Whittock.
  The format of  Who Wants To Be A Millionaire? (WWTBAM) was groundbreaking at the time it was first aired in 1998. The multiple-choice questions start incredibly, laughably easy, but quickly ramp up in difficulty. So does the prize money. Question 4 is worth £500, and this number approximately doubles with each subsequent question (i.e. exponential growth), until by Question 15 the prize money is £1 million. One wrong answer though, and you lose.
  Her husband Major Charles Ingram subsequently appeared on the show in September 2001, in an appearance which spanned two nights of filming.
 On the first night he used up two lifelines and made it to £4,000 (Question 7), considered a pretty weak performance by the standards of the show.
 On the second night he did much better, managing to make it all the way to win £1 million, becoming only the 3rd contestant to ever do so.
 The production team suspected cheating during filming, a suspicion which deepened as they pursued their investigation.
 It was alleged that the Ingrams had conspired with a college lecturer called Tecwen Whittock, who would cough after the correct answer was said in order to help Charles Ingram out.
 In 2003 the case made it to the Crown Court ( R v Ingram, C., Ingram, D. and Whittock, T.) creating a tabloid frenzy.
 A recent ITV drama has sparked interest in the Ingrams and Whittock again. James Graham’s miniseries   Quiz  presents the Ingrams in a pretty favourable light, highlighting the problems with the legal case against them and some questions that remain unanswered to this day.
  Despite the British public and the courts having clearly made their mind up about the Ingrams’ guilt, Graham’s documentary raises the question: could this actually have been a nightmare scenario for the Ingrams, where a respectable couple’s reputation was dragged through the mud by a vindictive and bitter production team who built up a plausible story on flimsy evidence?
 I decided to w̶a̶s̶t̶e̶ use my knowledge of statistics to help determine the truth.
   In the trial, the defence argued that the case was extremely unusual because Celador, the production company who made  WWTBAM, had provided the recording which formed the crucial evidence, rather than the police. This evidence took the form of ‘Tape G’, a version of the episode where they had amplified the background recording mikes such that extremely loud coughs could be heard. As far as I’m aware, the only recordings available online are all descendants of ‘Tape G’ i.e. they all have the alleged cheating coughs amplified in the mix.
 A key fact mentioned by the defence team was that there were actually 192 total coughs over the course of the recording. I’m not sure how long the recording actually lasted, but this is clearly meant to sound like a big number and put the amplified coughs into context. As Wikipedia has it:
 A sound analyst affirmed that 192 coughs were heard during the filming and the sound supervisor said 19 “significant” coughs had come from near a live microphone which he believed to be one of the FFF microphones on the side where Whittock was sitting. ( link)
 Interestingly, Whittock didn’t deny that he had coughed at these moments. His defence was that even if he had, it was a  coincidence that those coughs had happened when the correct answers did. Indeed, the defence presented several key witnesses who gave evidence that Whittock had a recurrent cough, suggesting he couldn’t control when he coughed.
 So, how likely is it that the “significant” coughs happened when they did? Could they just have happened randomly at those times, and then been amplified? (I’m assuming that Celador would not stoop so low as to manipulate the footage by adding entirely new coughs.)
 To understand how likely or unlikely events like these are, a useful starting point is the  Poisson distribution. This assumes that events are independent (one cough doesn’t depend on another) and happen at a uniform mean rate. So if we know that on average there are 3 coughs every minute, we can calculate the probability of getting 5 coughs in a given minute.
 The Poisson distribution has been used in many classical statistical analyses, from the analysis of deaths due to  horse kicks in the Prussian Army (appropriately for Major Ingram) to the distribution of  flying bombs over London.
  Filming on the second night lasted for 25 minutes, as per Ben Crowl’s video (this is certainly an underestimate, but we’re trying to be as generous as possible to Whittock: a shorter time is better for his defence because it gives him a higher coughing rate, and thus a higher probability of coughs appearing in a given interval)
 Whittock coughs randomly and independently at a mean rate of 19/25 = 0.76 coughs per minute (cpm) over filming.
 From Question 8 onwards, the correct answer to a question — not counting when it is said after “final answer” has been locked in — is said a total of 62 times.
 Ingram is responsible for 48 of these; 14 are said by Chris Tarrant. (This is because he always says every answer at least once when he reads out the question and the answers; sometimes he repeats them later.)
 Let’s assume that a cough in a five-second period after the answer would be quite useful to Charles Ingram, if that indeed was the (alleged) plan. However, to be fair, of those 62 correct answers some of these are said in quick succession. So let’s only remove the cases where where a correct answer was said within five seconds of the last time the correct answer was said. That happens 20 times. So that leaves 42 times the correct answer was said over the course of filming.
 So there is a total duration of 42 x 5 seconds, during which Whittock admits coughing 19 times. This would be an average cough rate of 5.42 cpm, which is about  7 times higher than the average coughing rate we were assuming. So it already doesn’t look good for Whittock.
 However, to give us an idea of the overall statistics, let’s run some simulations under the assumption of the Poisson distribution. How likely is it to get 19 coughs in 42 ‘trials’ (5-second period after a correct answer)?
 We can do that by simulating the Poisson distribution with given parameters for 10,000 simulations. In other words, imagine 10,000 separate filmings of  WWTBAM that Whittock attends and coughs randomly and independently with his usual cough rate, before nefarious sound editors at Celador have their way with the resulting footage. In how many of those does it look bad for him, with at least 19 coughs in the 5-second periods after answers?
  The figure above shows us that the probability of the producers having 19 coughs to play with is…vanishingly small. Notice that the x-axis doesn’t even make it up to 19! The most coughs ever seen in those periods in one of our imaginary filmings is 10. So the probability of 19 coughs at these times is <1 in 10,000 — under the assumption that Whittock’s 19 total coughs were random and independent.
  At this point, if I was the Ingrams’ defence lawyer, I would counter back: “Your Honour, just because we’ve shown that Whittock’s coughing is very unlikely to be Poisson distributed, that doesn’t mean it was part of a plan to cheat. Yes, we may now have strong evidence that the coughing is not of a uniform rate and coughs are not independent of each other, but what were we expecting? Coughing isn’t really uniform, it can vary over time. Indeed, a crucial part of our defence is that as filming progressed Whittock’s throat got drier and his coughing increased. Nor is it independent: as recurrent cough sufferers know, one cough can spark other coughs. And is it not possible that at moments of high tension we should expect more coughing?
 All good points. But it does make the coincidence defence seem pretty implausible — for most definitions of ‘coincidence’.
  If we assume that  half of all 192 coughs were Whittock, giving a cough rate of ~5 times the rate we previously assumed, then (unsurprisingly) the probability of 19 coughs in 42 trials becomes a bit more favourable:
  In some simulations, there are even more than 19 coughs in the 42 trials. In fact, if we sum up the total probabilities in this (quite generous) scenario, the probability of getting at least 19 coughs in the 42 trials is  7.82%.
 In other words, if Whittock really was a super-cougher throughout  WWTBAM, then about ~8% of episodes he was in the audience for (assuming the contestant made it all the way to Question 15) would probably contain 19 or more coughs after the correct answers from Question 8 onwards.
 This is still not that likely, but it’s definitely in the realm of statistically plausible. (A common threshold used to decide “significant” results is 5%, so by that threshold we would conclude this isn’t significant.) Remember though, that this is assuming that Whittock coughed 96 times during filming. That’s a lot.
 EDIT:  Since writing this originally, I’ve found a   contemporary account  which claims that Ingram spent   50 minutes  answering on the second night of filming, and mentions   36 total coughs  from Whittock (although states it’s unproven who did these coughs, nor the 19 “significant” ones). Assuming a cough rate of 36/50=0.72 cpm and running the simulations, the results still aren’t good for Whittock: still <1 in 10,000 chance of those 19 coughs happening in the 42 ‘correct answer + five seconds’ trials under the Poisson distribution.
 OK, so much for statistical arguments from Whittock’s overall numbers of coughs. Ideally we’d look at the  actual pattern of  all the coughs, unbiased by whether it’s (supposedly) Whittock or somebody else.
 I hear your concern: but to do that we’d need a matched dataset of coughs and correct answers with resolution to the nearest second. Such a thing doesn’t exist!
   In an ideal world, we would simply go back to the ‘original’ recording of the episode and analyse it carefully. This was my original plan. What better way to spend a day in lockdown?
 I encountered difficulty straight away even finding a consistent recording to watch the entire episode on. For example, the official  WWTBAM Youtube channel put out a video called  “Charles Ingram Fraud Scandal” on April 17, the description of which claimed “I t’s finally HERE! The real footage of Charles Ingram playing millionaire, now you can watch and decide for yourself!”.
 I tried to use this episode for analysis, but to put it mildly, this edit could reasonably be described as ‘propaganda’. The footage is frequently interrupted by cut screens telling you what to look out for, and at several points footage is paused, rewound, and replayed. Not the easiest to record exact timings of coughs and answers for statistical analysis.
 Fortunately,  WWTBAM super-fan and Youtube user Ben Crowl (his channel is called “The Home of Millionaire”) had seamlessly spliced together all 15 questions together into a  single edit which he uploaded on April 19.
 I quickly sat down and got to work. I watched the episode through playing audio through headphones, with the volume turned up, and no video (to avoid distractions, and the fact that Celador used the edit to present a particular narrative) and logged every single cough I heard. I then went back and double-checked every cough. I also recorded every single time that Chris Tarrant or Charles Ingram said *any* of the answers to the questions from Question 8 onwards (when the coughing plan is alleged to have started). These timings are all accurate to within a second.
 (It was at this point that I wondered whether I was really coping as well with life during lockdown as I had thought.)
 I’m fairly confident this represents the most comprehensive cough-time log dataset relating to the Ingram episode. Unfortunately, when I went back to check the video on April 21 the video was unavailable in the UK for copyright reasons. ( EDIT: it seems to be back up as of April 24).
  Unfortunately, absolute times of coughs etc. as I refer to them in this video are going to be different depending on whichever Youtube clip you manage to find (and there are quite a few). But you should be able to line everything up whichever video you watch using the first four answers of the question, when Chris Tarrant reads them out.
 There are obvious problems with this analysis. For example, the music is louder in the earlier questions so it’s harder to hear coughs than in the later questions, and as noted the audio has been  very manipulated. But for now, let’s work with the data we have (a common problem for statisticians). We can now go through the Ingram episode question-by-question in unprecedented detail, maybe a level of detail not seen since  R v Ingram, C., Ingram, D. and Whittock, T. itself.
    I’m first going to go through on a question-by-question level, starting with Question 8, since that’s the second night of filming when the “significant” coughing is meant to have started. To remain agnostic about the identity of the cougher, I’ve treated all coughs the same. If it was remotely audible, it’s in here, and treated identically.
 In total, there are 64 coughs and 143 stated answers from Question 8 onwards. (This seems about right if there were 192 coughs in total over the whole episode and we’re only watching a set of edited clips in this analysis.)
 In the plots below, to make coughs visible I’ve added a 1 second width; the cough happens at the start of the vertical grey bar. Answers are shown as short vertical lines, with the correct answer in red. Where Tarrant said the answer rather than Ingram, there’s a small ‘T’. The “significant” coughs will appear just to the right of correct answers.
 (If statistics/coughing aren’t your thing, there’s also some   Non-coughing trivia related to each question.)
   Straight away, we see something that might help the defence out. There are actually some coughs which happen immediately after somebody says the  wrong  answer! For example, immediately after “Ronald Reagan” is said by Ingram, there’s a cough. They aren’t amplified in the mix by as much as the “significant” coughs chosen by Celador, but they’re still audible. Who knows, maybe this would have been equally audible in the studio? Unfortunately we simply don’t know.
 Ingram seems immediately fairly confident that it’s Aristotle Onassis and breezes through this question.
 Non-coughing trivia:  the prospect of a marriage between Ronald Reagan and Jacqueline Kennedy is briefly raised. Kennedy was 39 when she married Aristotle Onassis (b.1906) in 1968; Reagan was 57 (b.1911)— a full 5 years younger than Aristotle Onassis.
   It’s not a particularly difficult question for Ingram. Again, he seems confident from the start that it’s Switzerland. There is a cough almost simultaneously with when he says Switzerland (the correct answer). He does then rattle through all the answers, none of which are followed by coughs.
 Non-coughing trivia : Ingram claims he must have eaten Emmenthal “a hundred times”. He was 38 when filming took place, so assuming that he only ate Emmenthal as an adult that’s about 5 times a year. Seems likely.
   This question is one of the key questions in the episode. Ingram spends a long time on it, admitting straight away that he has no idea what the correct answer is. He uses up his remaining lifeline (a 50:50) and is left with answers C and D, hence Tarrant repeating them just before the 16 minute mark, which he then ricochets between. He says he’ll go for A1, admitting that he’s “never heard of Craig David”, before changing his mind just after 17 minutes.
 If you watch the episode, you’ll notice that some of the “significant” coughs are from Diana Ingram. I haven’t shown this information on the graph because we’re (trying) to remain agnostic about the identity of the coughers. Notably,  the phantom cougher didn’t cough at all in this question. The prosecution argued that “easy pop culture” was not necessarily easy for quiz aficionados such as Whittock.
 Non-coughing trivia : Ingram claims he’s never heard of “ColdPLAY”, putting the stress on the second syllable, which sounds bizarre and suggests he’s telling the truth. Also, I found hearing two men saying “Craig David” repeatedly quite funny and was surprised the audience didn’t giggle. But   ‘Bo' Selecta! ’  didn’t air until September 2002, so that incredibly annoying catchphrase was yet to come. When Whittock appeared on his own episode after Ingram, he also struggled with pop culture: he   couldn’t  identify “Jabba the Hutt” as the true Star Wars character in a list of other fake names (e.g. Gabba the Shak)  for his £2,000 question, and had to ask the audience.
   A fairly cough-sparse but answer-rich question. Tarrant repeats the answers about halfway through, even though Ingram interrupts him. There are some “significant” coughs after “cricket”. However, Ingram seems quite confident (less so than on Questions 8 and 9).
 Non-coughing trivia : Ingram uses the argument that he recalls Gentlemen vs. Players being associated with cricket from cigarette cards. My guess is that he’s probably thinking of   Player’s cigarettes , which did famously feature general interest cigarette cards — including some cricket-related ones. But I don’t think there’s actually any explicit connection to the annual   cricket match . Still, it apparently got him to the right answer.
   An early cough after Van Eyck — unusual because people seem to control their coughing when Tarrant is reading. Lots of coughs happen after Holbein, but only some of these are amplified phantom coughs. Some appear to be ‘collateral damage’ coughs, possible caused by people suddenly feeling the urge to cough themselves after (subconsciously) hearing a cough. (The effect can be quite strong: I just coughed after writing that.) Ingram seems to know it’s either Holbein or Rembrandt.
 Non-coughing trivia : Ingram says that he’s seen the famous   painting . (Weirdly to me, Wikipedia takes you to the 1903   Henry James novel  if you use ‘The Ambassadors’ alone, which seems much more obscure than Holbein’s painting, but there we are.) Ingram doesn’t say if he stood at the correct position to see the skull resolved. In fact, most people standing in the National Gallery put their head in the wrong place — if you’re ever bored in Trafalgar Square, going in to watch people queuing up expecting to be amazed and then shrugging and walking away confused. It’s an ongoing piece of performance art. But if you stand with your head in   exactly  the right place, it is an incredible illusion. Alexander Boxer has made an   interesting video  about the painting where he reconstructs the illusion with uncanny precision.

note_en:
Taking notes is a part of my life and work: jot down ideas, manage tasks and track personal projects.But soon came a problem, what tool is best for supporting this simple task. There are 3 must-have features I searched for:   As for me, todo note or shopping list is too trivial. And editing markdown on smart phone sucks and inconvenient. So I just focus on PC and laptop station here. I found  Boostnote first. Free as it’s a FOSS (Free and Open Source Software). But this fancy editor is (as expected) built on  Electron, a RAM-hungry framework. No built-in sync feature, but enabled through third party like Google Drive or Dropbox. Since I didn’t like the roundabout syncing, so  Inkdrop is my second stop. Also built on Electron, but came with built-in sync service through  CouchDB, and charge you less than $5 per month. This was pretty suitable for me, and I felt comfortable in 2-month trial. The creator also wrote a  post about process of building his product, which is very interesting to know about. But I don’t really like the idea of a RAM-killer app for just a simple task like saving texts. There should be another ways for rubbing my itch.  Vim was my main editor, until I knew about  Org-mode, which is only fully available on Emacs. Vim was good, but I’m somewhat not in the mood of learning Vimscript for long-term scaling my  .vimrc. In contrast, I love Lisp - the good old legend survived through time.So  Emacs Lisp +  Org-mode are 2-main reasons made me make a switch to a new life-long partner: Emacs. So after grabbing the basics and setting a somewhat decent  .emacs.d, fully integrating Org-mode, life is damn good. ;; [...];; simple setup(require-package 'org)(add-hook 'org-mode-hook 'turn-on-font-lock)(global-set-key "\C-cl" 'org-store-link)(global-set-key "\C-ca" 'org-agenda)(global-set-key "\C-cc" 'org-capture)(global-set-key "\C-cb" 'org-iswitchb)(setq org-log-done 'time);; Turn off auto-fold(setq org-startup-folded nil);; [...]
 My dotfiles’s  .emacs.d for your information. And a good setup  guide for Emacs in my opinion. Org mode is for keeping notes, maintaining TODO lists, planning projects, and authoring documents with a fast and effective plain-text system.-  org-mode
 Talk is cheap, actually use this with powerful key combination really broads my horizon. For example:  Besides, learning Org syntax is not so hard, from a perspective of a Markdown fan-boy like me.  So Emacs + Org-mode is totally free, check. Wonderful Markdown/Org editor, check. It occurred to me that I knew about  Syncthing in a Golang blog post: Syncthing is an open-source cross platform peer-to-peer continuous file synchronization application.-  Eight years of Go
 Basically, Syncthing decentralizes your shared data to all connected nodes identified with cryptographic certificates. I need at least a live node for no-downtime syncing (a central hub). A low cost private cloud server is a good choice, but in my case I used my 99.99% uptime Raspberry Pi 3 (which mainly ran  Pi-hole for my home). # Add GPG keywget -O - https://syncthing.net/release-key.txt | sudo apt-key add -# Add repositoryecho "deb http://apt.syncthing.net/ syncthing release" | sudo tee -a /etc/apt/sources.list.d/syncthing-release.list# Update and installsudo apt-get updatesudo apt-get install syncthing -y
 Re-bind configuration from local loopback  127.0.0.1 to  0.0.0.0 ( /home/pi/.config/syncthing/config.xml):  This will enable you to access  http://<pi's IP>:8384 Web Interface of Syncthing. Finally just start  syncthing service:  Access  http://<pi's IP>:8384: Here you can see I shared my  /home/pi/org directory which contains all my  .org notes to 2 nodes: online  dell + offline  thinkpad. Now if I make a change from my  /home/dell/org, it will syncs back to  /home/pi/org. And when my  thinkpad goes online,  /home/thinkpad/org auto updates all its notes, too! Et Voilà! Work like a charm. Plus, both Emacs and Syncthing use just a little of RAM and CPU resource.  Inkdrop solo, (Vim + Git) traditional way, or (Emacs + Org-mode + Syncthing) combo with hands-free syncing? Use the right tool for the job, and pick what suits you the best. For now, Emacs is my everyday editor, including programming stuff and note-taking. Long live (Emacs) Lisp.

在编辑“容器如何工作”杂志的功能页面时，我发现自己在试图解释为什么strace在Docker容器中不能工作。
这里的问题是-如果在Docker容器中运行strace，则会发生以下情况：
$docker run-it ubuntu：18.04/bin/bash$#.。安装strace.。[电子邮件受保护]：/#strace lsstrace：ptrace(ptrace_TRACEME，.)：不允许操作。
strace使用ptrace系统调用工作，所以如果不允许使用ptrace，它肯定不会工作！这个很容易修复-在我的机器上，这个可以修复它：
但我对修复它不感兴趣，我想知道为什么会发生这种情况。那么，为什么strace不工作，为什么--cap-add=SYS_ptrace可以修复它呢？
我一直认为原因是Docker容器进程by Default没有CAP_SYS_PTRACE功能。这与通过--cap-add=SYS_ptrace进行修复是一致的，对吗？
原因1：实验上，作为一个普通用户，我可以依赖于myuser运行的任何进程。但是，如果我检查当前进程是否具有CAP_SYS_PTRACE功能，则不会：
因此，CAP_SYS_PTRACE的目的是让您ptrace任何用户拥有的任意进程，就像root用户通常可以做到的那样。您不应该仅仅为了ptrace您的用户拥有的常规进程而需要它。
我用第三种方法测试了这一点-我用dockerrun--cap-add=SYS_ptrace-it ubuntu：18.04/bin/bash运行了一个Docker容器，删除了CAP_SYS_ptrace功能，即使我不再拥有该功能，我仍然可以加速进程。什么？为什么？
我的下一个假设(没有那么充分的依据)大致是这样的：“嗯，可能进程在不同的用户命名空间中，并且因为…，所以策略不起作用。原因是什么？“。这并不是真正连贯的，但当我调查它的时候就发生了这样的事情。
因为用户命名空间ID(4026531837)相同，所以容器中的根用户与主机上的根用户完全相同。因此，它绝对没有理由不能阻止它创建的进程！
这个假设没有多大意义，但我没有意识到Docker容器中的根用户与主机上的根用户相同，所以我认为这很有趣。
我还知道Docker使用seccomp-bpf来阻止容器进程运行大量系统调用。并且ptrace在被Docker的默认seccompprofile阻止的系统调用列表中！(实际上，允许的系统调用的列表是一个白名单，所以只是ptrace不在默认的白名单中。但结果却是一样的。)。
这很容易解释为什么strace在Docker容器中不能工作-如果ptrace系统调用被完全阻塞，那么当然您根本无法调用它，strace就会失败。
让我们验证这个假设-如果我们禁用所有seccomp规则，是否可以在Docker容器中进行westrace？
$docker run--security-opt seccomp=unconfined-it ubuntu：18.04/bin/bash$strace lexecve(&#34；/bin/ls&#34；，[&#34；ls&#34；]，0x7ffc69a65580/*8 vars*/)=0.。它工作得很好..。
这和Seccomp规则没有任何关系！到底怎么回事？
当文档没有帮助时，唯一能做的就是查看源代码。
Go的好处是，因为依赖项通常在AGO存储库中提供，所以您只需对存储库执行grep，就可以找出执行某项操作的代码在哪里。所以我克隆了github.com/moby/moby和greppedfor一些内容，比如rgCAP_SYS_ptrace。
这就是我认为正在发生的事情。在tainerd的seccomp实现中，在contrib/seccomp/seccomp_default.go中，有一组代码确保如果一个进程具有可访问性，那么它也被授予访问权限(通过seccomp规则)来使用与该功能相关的系统调用。
案例&#34；CAP_SYS_PTRACE&#34；：s.Syscalls=APPEND(s.Syscalls，specs.LinuxSyscall{Names：[]string{&#34；kcmp&#34；，&#34；process_VM_readv&#34；，&#34；process_VM_Writev&#34；，&#34；ptrace&#34；，}，Action：specs.ActAllow，args：[]specs.Linuxx。
在profile/seccomp/seccomp.goin Moby和默认的seccompprofile中，还有一些其他代码似乎做了一些非常相似的事情，所以有可能就是这样做的。
结果似乎是--cap-add并不完全像手册页中所说的那样，它更像是--cap-add-and-also-whitelist-some-extra-system-calls-if-required.。这让我感觉到了！如果您有CAP_SYS_PTRACE这样的功能，该功能理应允许您使用PROCESS_VM_READV系统调用，但该系统调用被seccomp配置文件阻止，这对您没有太大帮助！
因此，当您给容器CAP_SYS_PTRACE时，允许PROCESS_VM_READV和ptrace系统调用似乎是一个合理的选择。
在这次提交时(docker 19.03)，Docker实际上允许ptrace系统调用比4.8更新的内核版本。
这是一件有趣的小事，我认为这是一个很好的例子，说明了容器是如何由许多移动的部件组成的，这些部件以不完全明显的方式协同工作。
note_en:
While editing the capabilities page of the  how containers work zine, I found myselftrying to explain why  strace doesn’t work in a Docker container.
 The problem here is – if you run  strace in a Docker container, this happens:
 $ docker run -it ubuntu:18.04 /bin/bash$ # ... install strace ... [email protected]:/# strace lsstrace: ptrace(PTRACE_TRACEME, ...): Operation not permitted
 strace works using the  ptrace system call, so if  ptrace isn’tallowed, it’s definitely not gonna work! This is pretty easy to fix – onmy machine, this fixes it:
  But I wasn’t interested in fixing it, I wanted to know why it happens. Sowhy does strace not work, and why does  --cap-add=SYS_PTRACE fix it?
  I always thought the reason was that Docker container processes bydefault didn’t have the  CAP_SYS_PTRACE capability. This is consistentwith it being fixed by  --cap-add=SYS_PTRACE, right?
  Reason 1: Experimentally, as a regular user, I can strace on any process run by myuser. But if I check if my current process has the  CAP_SYS_PTRACE capability, I don’t:
    So the point of  CAP_SYS_PTRACE is to let you ptrace  arbitraryprocesses owned by any user, the way that root usually can. You shouldn’tneed it to just ptrace a regular process owned by your user.
 And I tested this a third way – I ran a Docker container with  dockerrun --cap-add=SYS_PTRACE -it ubuntu:18.04 /bin/bash, dropped the CAP_SYS_PTRACE capability, and I could still strace processes eventhough I didn’t have that capability anymore. What? Why?
  My next (much less well-founded) hypothesis was something along the linesof “um, maybe the process is in a different user namespace and stracedoesn’t work because of… reasons?” This isn’t really coherent buthere’s what happened when I looked into it.
     Because the user namespace ID ( 4026531837) is the same, the root userin the container is the exact same user as the root user on the host. Sothere’s definitely no reason it shouldn’t be able to strace processesthat it created!
 This hypothesis doesn’t make much sense but I hadn’t realized that theroot user in a Docker container is the same as the root user on the host,so I thought that was interesting.
  I also knew that Docker uses seccomp-bpf to stop container processes fromrunning a lot of system calls. And ptrace is in the  list of system callsblocked by Docker’s default seccompprofile! (actually thelist of allowed system calls is a whitelist, so it’s just that ptrace isnot in the default whitelist. But it comes out to the same thing.)
 That easily explains why strace wouldn’t work in a Docker container – ifthe  ptrace system call is totally blocked, then of course you can’tcall it at all and strace would fail.
 Let’s verify this hypothesis – if we disable all seccomp rules, can westrace in a Docker container?
 $ docker run --security-opt seccomp=unconfined -it ubuntu:18.04 /bin/bash$ strace lsexecve("/bin/ls", ["ls"], 0x7ffc69a65580 /* 8 vars */) = 0... it works fine ...
      That doesn’t have anything to do with seccomp rules! What’s going on?
  When the documentation doesn’t help, the only thing to do is go look atthe source.
 The nice thing about Go is, because dependencies are often vendored in aGo repository, you can just grep the repository to figure out where thecode that does a thing is. So I cloned  github.com/moby/moby and greppedfor some things, like  rg CAP_SYS_PTRACE.
 Here’s what I think is going on. In containerd’s seccomp implementation, in contrib/seccomp/seccomp_default.go,there’s a bunch of code that makes sure that if a process has acapability, then it’s also given access (through a seccomp rule) to usethe system calls that go with that capability.
 case "CAP_SYS_PTRACE":			s.Syscalls = append(s.Syscalls, specs.LinuxSyscall{				Names: []string{					"kcmp",					"process_vm_readv",					"process_vm_writev",					"ptrace",				},				Action: specs.ActAllow,				Args: []specs.LinuxSeccompArg{},			})
 There’s some other code that seems to do something very similar in profiles/seccomp/seccomp.goin moby and the  default seccompprofile,so it’s possible that that’s what’s doing it instead.
   The upshot seems to be that  --cap-add doesn’t do exactly what it saysit does in the man page, it’s more like --cap-add-and-also-whitelist-some-extra-system-calls-if-required. Which makessense! If you have a capability like  CAP_SYS_PTRACE which is supposedto let you use the  process_vm_readv system call but that system call isblocked by a seccomp profile, that’s not going to help you much!
 So allowing the  process_vm_readv and  ptrace system calls when yougive the container  CAP_SYS_PTRACE seems like a reasonable choice.
  As of  this commit (docker 19.03), Docker does actually allow the  ptrace system calls for kernel versions newer than 4.8.
   This was a fun small thing to investigate, and I think it’s a niceexample of how containers are made of lots of moving pieces that worktogether in not-completely-obvious ways.
title: 为什么Strace在Docker中不起作用
title_en: Why strace doesn't work in Docker

编者按：“经济学人”正在向我们的每日通讯“今日经济学人”的读者免费提供一些关于冠状病毒大流行的最重要的报道。要接收它，请在这里注册。有关我们的冠状病毒跟踪器和更多报道，请参阅我们的中心。
以他的名字命名的小说中的恶棍A引用了芝加哥对詹姆斯·邦德的一句生动的格言：“一次是偶然，两次是巧合，第三次是敌人的行动。”
直到2002年，医学才知道有几种冠状病毒感染了人类，没有一种会导致严重疾病。然后，在2002年，一种现在被称为-o的病毒在中国广东省浮出水面。随后爆发的严重急性呼吸综合征(SARS)在得到控制之前导致全球774人死亡。2012年，另一种新疾病，中东呼吸综合征()，预示着-o的到来，虽然它没有传播得那么远和那么广泛(除了去韩国旅行)，但还没有被消除。到目前为止，它已经造成858人死亡，最近一次是在2月4日。
第三次是-o-2，现在导致22.5万人死于冠状病毒。-o和-o都与在野生蝙蝠中发现的冠状病毒密切相关。在-o的案例中，公认的故事是，病毒从云南省一个洞穴里的蝙蝠传播到了果子狸身上，这些果子狸在广东的市场上出售。在-o的案例中，病毒从蝙蝠传播到骆驼。现在它经常从骆驼传给人类，这使得它很难消除，但只有在非常接近的条件下才在人与人之间传播，这使得它是可控的。
蝙蝠中的一种起源似乎也极有可能是-o-2。然而，它从蝙蝠到人类的路线尚未确定。如果像-o一样，病毒仍在动物储藏室中传播，它可能会在未来再次爆发。如果没有，其他病毒肯定会尝试类似的东西。世界卫生组织的人畜共患病(从动物传染给人的疾病)专家彼得·本·恩巴雷克(Peter Ben Embark)表示，随着人类和他们饲养的动物进入与野生动物有更密切接触的新领域，这种溢出效应正变得越来越常见。了解这种溢出是如何发生的细节，应该会为阻止它们提供洞察力。
然而，在一些人的心目中，敌人采取行动的可能性隐约可见，因为有些东西比病毒更大。自从20世纪70年代基因工程出现以来，阴谋论者指出，几乎每一种新的传染病，从s到埃博拉，从莱姆病到寨卡，都是人类修修补补或恶意行为的结果。
冠状病毒大流行的政治意味着，这一次这样的理论比往常更有吸引力。大流行始于中国，在中国，政府掩盖问题的根深蒂固的冲动导致它推迟了可能遏制其传播的措施。它在美国造成了最大的死亡人数，那里记录的冠状病毒死亡人数已经超过了华盛顿越南战争纪念碑上的名字。
无论发生什么，这些事实都会导致太平洋彼岸响起指控之声。更糟糕的是，一些人怀疑-o-2可能在某种程度上与中国的病毒学研究有关，这样说可能会重新追究任何责任。
没有证据支持这一说法。西方专家断然表示，中国科学家很早就公开、准确地公布了这种新病毒的基因组序列，这揭示了基因工程不会给它留下任何蛛丝马迹。但事实仍然是，在首次发现疫情的武汉，有一个实验室，科学家们过去曾在那里故意使冠状病毒更具致病性。
这样的研究是在世界各地的实验室进行的。它的支持者将其视为研究冠状病毒如此残酷地带到聚光灯下的问题的重要方式：病毒是如何成为引发大流行的那种东西的？武汉病毒学研究所()做了一些这样的研究，看起来几乎肯定是巧合。然而，如果没有令人信服的关于疾病起源的替代解释，仍有怀疑的余地。
2003年疫情背后的病毒来源--一些病毒学家现在讽刺地称其为“经典”--在很大程度上是由施正力研究员确立的，她有时在中国媒体上被称为“蝙蝠夫人”。在一段时间里，她和她的团队走访了全国各地的偏远地区，寻找蝙蝠或它们鸟粪中的-o的近亲。他们在云南一个满是马蹄蝠的山洞里发现了一只。
正是在这些研究过程中收集的病毒基因组中，科学家们现在发现了与O最接近的蝙蝠病毒。云南省同一洞穴中聚集的一种名为a13的毒株与这种新病毒的基因序列有96%的同源性。A13不是该病毒的祖先。它更像是它的表亲。悉尼大学的病毒学家爱德华·霍姆斯(Edward Holmes)估计，两者之间4%的差异代表着与一些共同的先例至少20年的进化分歧，可能更多的是50年左右。
尽管从理论上说，蝙蝠可能已经将一种从前身直接传给人类的病毒传染给了人类，但专家们发现这个想法不太可能。蝙蝠病毒看起来与-o-2病毒有特定的不同之处。在-o-2中，病毒颗粒表面的刺突蛋白有一个受体结合域()，它擅长粘附到病毒感染的人类细胞表面的特定分子上。蝙蝠体内的冠状病毒是不一样的。
最近的一项研究表明-o-2是天然基因组重组的产物。感染同一宿主的不同冠状病毒非常乐意交换基因组片段。如果一种类似于13的蝙蝠病毒进入一种已经感染了冠状病毒的动物体内，这种冠状病毒号称更适合感染人类，那么很可能会出现一种基本上更适合人类的狂热病毒。这就是-O-2的样子。
早些时候，人们普遍认为中间宿主很可能是武汉华南海鲜野生动物市场出售的一个物种，在那里，从浣熊到雪貂，从近到远，各种动物都挤在不卫生的条件下。许多早期的人类冠状病毒病例都与这个市场有关。乔纳森·爱泼斯坦(Jonathan Epstein)是生态健康联盟(EcoHealth Alliance，An)的科学副总裁，他说，在市场上585个不同表面的棉签中，约有33个-o-2呈阳性。他们都来自以出售野生动物著称的地区。这几乎是间接证据所能提供的最有力的证据。
第一个受到严重怀疑的动物是穿山甲。在穿山甲中发现的一种冠状病毒与-o-2的病毒基本相同，这表明它可能是蝙蝠病毒在变成-o-2的过程中与之重组的病毒。穿山甲被用于传统医学，虽然它们是濒危的，但在菜单上仍然可以找到它们。显然没有它们在华南市场交易的记录。但考虑到这种交易是非法的，而且这样的记录现在看起来相当有罪，这很难证明它们不是非法的。
已知穿山甲携带病毒，而-o-2可能从中感染了与人类相容的病毒，这一事实无疑是有启发意义的。但是一系列其他动物也可能携带这样的病毒；只是科学家们还没有彻底研究所有的病毒。In-O-2不仅用于攻击人类的细胞，而且可能还用于攻击穿山甲。它也提供了进入其他物种中类似细胞的途径。最近几周，人们发现-o-2已经从人类进化到了家猫、饲养的水貂和老虎。有一些证据表明它实际上可以在猫之间传播，这使得人们可以想象它们是中间人-尽管到目前为止还没有证据表明猫感染了人类。
市场作为武汉疫情背后的人类感染场所的吸引力仍然很强；广东的一个市场被指责为疫情传播的罪魁祸首。然而，如果没有已知的中间体，对它不利的证据仍然是间接的。虽然许多早期人类病例与市场有关，但许多病例与市场无关。他们可能以一种未知的方式与市场有联系的人联系在一起。但谁也不能肯定。
在早期患者中发现的病毒基因组如此相似，以至于强烈表明病毒只从中间宿主传播给人一次。根据基因组分化的速度估计，这种转移的最早时间是2019年10月初。如果这是正确的，那么在12月初武汉出现第一例官方病例之前，几乎可以肯定的是，在武汉出现第一例官方病例之前，几乎可以肯定的是，有一些感染并不严重，或者没有到达医院，或者没有被认为是奇怪的。这些早期的案例可能发生在其他地方。
纽约哥伦比亚大学(Columbia University)感染和免疫中心主任伊恩·利普金(Ian Lipkin)正在与中国研究人员合作，检测去年年底从中国各地肺炎患者身上采集的血液样本，看看是否有证据表明病毒已经从其他地方传播到武汉。如果有，那么它进入华南市场可能不是关在笼子里，而是用两条腿。这个市场既受到游客的欢迎，也受到当地人的欢迎，而且靠近汉口火车站，汉口火车站是中国高铁网络的枢纽。
进一步的研究可能会使病毒何时、何地和如何进入人体变得更加清楚。在更广泛的可能的中间物种中，还有更多的病毒搜寻的余地。恩巴雷克博士说，如果有可能对那些最早感染冠状病毒的人进行详细的访谈，那么基因采样就可以更有针对性，如果运气好的话，可能会找到源头。但他补充说，这样做所需的时间“可能很快，也可能非常长”。
如果它被证明起源于其他地方，那么这种新病毒在武汉疫情早期阶段的识别可能会被证明要归功于这座城市集中的病毒学技术诀窍，这些技术现在肯定正在被投入到对来自更多来源的更多病毒进行测序中。但是，在对自然溢出做出令人满意的解释之前，武汉疾病预防控制中心和当地另一家研究中心的同样集中的技术诀窍将继续引起人们的怀疑。
2017年，中国第一家生物安全级别为4(-4)的实验室开业--这是一种高遏制设施，用于研究最危险的病原体。史博士在那里的很大一部分后期研究的目的是了解仍然在蝙蝠中传播的病毒有没有可能蔓延到人类群体中。在一次实验中，她和同样来自美国和意大利的葛兴义与美国和意大利的科学家合作，通过将蝙蝠冠状病毒014-o的基因组与感染老鼠的冠状病毒的基因组进行重组，探索了蝙蝠冠状病毒类似疾病的潜力。2015年11月的时事通讯报道说，由此产生的病毒可以“在原代人类气道细胞中高效复制，并在体外达到相当于O型流感流行株的滴度”。4月初，这份时事通讯和所有其他通讯都从该研究所的网站上删除了。
这项研究的结果也发表在“自然医学”上，它证明了-o从蝙蝠跳到人类并不是偶然的；其他蝙蝠冠状病毒也有类似的能力。知道这一点很有用。但是，赋予病原体和潜在病原体额外的力量，以了解它们可能有什么能力，这是一项有争议的工作。他们的支持者坚持认为，这些“获得功能”的实验有重要的用途，比如了解抗药性和病毒用来逃避免疫系统的伎俩。它们还带有明显的风险：它们所依赖的技术可能被滥用；它们的产品可能会泄漏。2011年，为了了解导致1918-19年大流行的流感毒株的特殊毒力，制造了一种增强的禽流感毒株，这引起了广泛的警觉。美国
伦敦国王学院(King‘s College)生物医学和安全专业的菲利帕·伦佐斯(Filippa Lentzos)表示，生物安全领域正在广泛讨论-o-2起源于合法研究的可能性。推测的可能性包括实验室的材料泄漏，以及人类在工作过程中意外感染，无论是在实验室还是在野外。
来自实验室的泄漏，包括-4个实验室，并不是闻所未闻的。世界上最后一个已知的天花病例是由1978年英国一家实验室的泄漏引起的。2007年爆发的口蹄疫也有类似的原因。在美国，涉及埃博拉病毒的意外泄漏和处理不当，以及从较低级别的实验室传来的一种致命的禽流感毒株。在中国，实验室工作人员似乎至少有两次被感染并将其传播给外部接触者。
毫无疑问，生物安全水平较低的实验室也会发生泄漏。但他们这样做了多少还不得而知，部分原因是人们对他们的担忧较少。就像在这个故事的其他部分一样，未知是一个培养皿，投机可以在其中滋生。这可能是人们对武汉疾病预防控制中心实验室感兴趣的部分原因。两名中国科学家在ResearchGate网站上发表的一份预印本随后被删除，这表明那里所做的工作可能令人担忧。据报道，该实验室饲养了动物，其中一项研究包括来自湖北和浙江的数百只蝙蝠，并专门收集病原体。
加州米德尔伯里国际问题研究所(Middlebury Institute Of International Studies)从事化学和生物武器防扩散工作的理查德·皮尔奇(Richard Pilch)表示，可以想象，这种新病毒的一个特征可能是在“传代实验”中出现的，即病原体在宿主之间传播，以研究它们传播能力的进化。这是“多碱基裂解位点”，可能会增强传染性。-o-2在其尖峰蛋白上有这样一个位点。它在蝙蝠冠状病毒中的近亲没有。但是，虽然这样的裂解位点可能是通过传代产生的，但没有证据表明，在这种情况下，它确实出现了。当病毒从一个宿主传播到另一个宿主时，它也可能以正常的方式进化。同时，福尔摩斯博士说，“没有证据表明-o-2……起源于中国武汉的一个实验室。”尽管其他人对巧合和可能性进行了猜测，但到目前为止，还没有人能够破坏这一说法。
许多科学家认为，随着如此多的生物学家积极寻找蝙蝠病毒，获得功能的工作变得越来越普遍，世界在某个时候发生实验室衍生的大流行的风险越来越大。皮尔奇博士说：“我对这场大流行最大的希望之一是我们能解决这个问题--这真的让我很担心。”今天，在30个国家大约有70-4个地点。当局计划兴建更多这类设施。
不过，再说一次，有必要考虑未知的情况。世界各地每年都有数以万计的呼吸系统疾病致死病例，其原因不明。其中一些可能是未被识别的人畜共患病的结果。这些威胁是否真的存在，以及这些威胁可能会如何累积，这一问题值得关注。这种关注需要实验室。它还需要一定程度的公开合作，而美国现在正在通过指责和削减资金来降低这种合作，而中国已经采取措施从源头上进行压制。这种压制对国家没有任何帮助；事实上，通过支持投机，它可能会损害国家。。
深入挖掘：有关冠状病毒大流行的最新报道，请注册我们的每日时事通讯“今日经济学人”，或访问我们的冠状病毒跟踪器和故事中心。
这篇文章发表在印刷版的科技版上，标题为“只连接”。

Editor’s note: The Economist  is making some of its most important coverage of the covid-19 pandemic freely available to readers of The Economist Today , our daily newsletter. To receive it, register   here. For our coronavirus tracker and more coverage, see our   hub
 A , villain of the novel which bears his name, quotes a vivid Chicago aphorism to James Bond: “Once is happenstance, twice is coincidence, the third time it’s enemy action.”
  Until 2002 medical science knew of a handful of coronaviruses that infected human beings, none of which caused serious illness. Then, in 2002, a virus now called  - o  surfaced in the Chinese province of Guangdong. The subsequent outbreak of severe acute respiratory syndrome ( ) killed 774 people around the world before it was brought under control. In 2012 another new illness, Middle Eastern respiratory syndrome ( ), heralded the arrival of  - o , which while not spreading as far and as wide as   (bar an excursion to South Korea) has not yet been eliminated. It has killed 858 people to date, the most recent of them on February 4th.
 The third time, it was  - o -2, now responsible for 225,000 covid-19 deaths. Both  - o  and  - o  are closely related to coronaviruses found in wild bats. In the case of  - o , the accepted story is that the virus spread from bats in a cave in Yunnan province into civets, which were sold at markets in Guangdong. In the case of  - o , the virus spread from bats into camels. It now passes regularly from camels to humans, which makes it hard to eliminate, but only spreads between people in conditions of close proximity, which makes it manageable.
  An origin among bats seems overwhelmingly likely for  - o -2, too. The route it took from bat to human, though, has yet to be identified. If, like  - o , the virus is still circulating in an animal reservoir, it could break out again in the future. If not, some other virus will surely try something similar. Peter Ben Embarek, an expert on zoonoses (diseases passed from animals to people) at the World Health Organisation says that such spillovers are becoming more common as humans and their farmed animals push into new areas where they have closer contact with wildlife. Understanding the detail of how such spillovers occur should provide insights into stopping them.
 In some minds, though, the possibility looms of enemy action on the part of something larger than a virus. Since the advent of genetic engineering in the 1970s, conspiracy theorists have pointed to pretty much every new infectious disease, from  s to Ebola to   to Lyme disease to   to Zika, as being a result of human tinkering or malevolence.
  The politics of the covid-19 pandemic mean that this time such theories have an even greater appeal than normal. The pandemic started in China, where the government’s ingrained urge to cover problems up led it to delay measures that might have curtailed its spread. It has claimed its greatest toll in America, where the recorded number of covid-19 deaths already outstrips the number of names on the Vietnam War Memorial in Washington,  .
 These facts would have led to accusations ringing out across the Pacific come what may. What makes things worse is a suspicion in some quarters that  - o -2 might in some way be connected to Chinese virological research, and that saying so may reapportion any blame.
 There is no evidence for the claim. Western experts say categorically that the sequence of the new virus’s genome—which Chinese scientists published early on, openly and accurately—reveals none of the telltales genetic engineering would leave in its wake. But it remains a fact that in Wuhan, where the outbreak was first spotted, there is a laboratory where scientists have in the past deliberately made coronaviruses more pathogenic.
 Such research is carried out in laboratories around the world. Its proponents see it as a vital way of studying the question that covid-19 has brought so cruelly into the spotlight: how does a virus become the sort of thing that starts a pandemic? That some of this research has been done at the Wuhan Institute of Virology ( ) seems all but certainly a coincidence. Without a compelling alternative account of the disease’s origin, however, there is room for doubt to remain.
   The origin of the virus behind the 2003   outbreak—“classic  ”, as some virologists now wryly call it—was established in large part by Shi Zhengli, a researcher at   sometimes referred to in Chinese media as “the bat lady”. Over a period of years she and her team visited remote locations all across the country in search of a close relative of  - o  in bats or their guano. They found one in a cave full of horseshoe bats in Yunnan.
 It is in the collection of viral genomes assembled during those studies that scientists have now found the bat virus closest to  - o . A strain called  a 13 gathered in the same cave in Yunnan shares 96% of its genetic sequence with the new virus.  a 13 is not that virus’s ancestor. It is something more like its cousin. Edward Holmes, a virologist at the University of Sydney, estimates that the 4% difference between the two represents at least 20 years of evolutionary divergence from some common antecedent, and probably something more like 50.
 Although bats could, in theory, have passed a virus descended from that antecedent directly to humans, experts find the idea unlikely. The bat viruses look different from  - o -2 in a specific way. In  - o -2 the spike protein on the viral particle’s surface has a receptor-binding domain ( ) that is adept at sticking to a particular molecule on the surface of the human cells the virus infects. The   in bat coronaviruses is not the same.
 One recent study suggests that  - o -2 is the product of natural genomic recombination. Different coronaviruses infecting the same host are more than happy to swap bits of genome. If a bat virus similar to  a 13 got into an animal already infected with a coronavirus which boasted an   better suited to infecting humans, a basically batty virus with a more human-attuned   might well arise. That is what  - o -2 looks like.
  Early on, it was widely imagined that the intermediate host was likely to be a species sold in Wuhan’s Huanan Seafood and Wildlife Market, a place where all sorts of creatures, from raccoon dogs to ferret badgers, and from near and far, are crammed together in unsanitary conditions. Many early human cases of covid-19 were associated with this market. Jonathan Epstein, vice-president of science with EcoHealth Alliance, an  , says of 585 swabs of different surfaces around the market, about 33 were positive for  - o -2. They all came from the area known to sell wild animals. That is pretty much as strong as circumstantial evidence gets.
 The first animal to come under serious suspicion was the pangolin. A coronavirus found in pangolins has an   essentially identical to that of  - o -2, suggesting that it might have been the virus with which the bat virus recombined on its way to becoming  - o -2. Pangolins are used in traditional medicine, and though they are endangered, they can nonetheless be found on menus. There are apparently no records of them being traded at the Huanan market. But given that such trading is illegal, and that such records would now look rather incriminating, this is hardly proof that they were not.
 The fact that pangolins are known to harbour viruses from which  - o -2 could have picked up its human-compatible   is certainly suggestive. But a range of other animals might harbour such viruses, too; it’s just that scientists have not yet looked all that thoroughly. The   in  - o -2 is useful not only for attacking the cells of human beings and, presumably, pangolins. It provides access to similar cells in other species, too. In recent weeks  - o -2 has been shown to have found its way from humans into domestic cats, farmed mink and a tiger. There is some evidence that it can actually pass between cats, which makes it conceivable that they were the intermediate—though there is as yet no evidence of a cat infecting a human.
  The market’s appeal as a site for the human infections behind the Wuhan outbreak remains strong; a market in Guangdong is blamed for the spread of  . Without a known intermediate, though, the evidence against it remains circumstantial. Though many early human cases were associated with the market, plenty were not. They may have been linked to people with ties to the market in ways not yet known. But one cannot be sure.
   The viral genomes found in early patients are so similar as to suggest strongly that the virus jumped from its intermediate host to people only once. Estimates based on the rate at which genomes diverge give the earliest time for this transfer as early October 2019. If that is right there were almost certainly infections which were not serious, or which did not reach hospitals, or which were not recognised as odd, before the first official cases were seen in Wuhan at the beginning of December. Those early cases may have taken place elsewhere.
 Ian Lipkin, the boss of the Centre for Infection and Immunity at Columbia University, in New York, is working with Chinese researchers to test blood samples taken late last year from patients with pneumonia all around China, to see if there is any evidence for the virus having spread to Wuhan from somewhere else. If there is, then it may have entered Huanan market not in a cage, but on two legs. The market is popular with visitors as well as locals, and is close to Hankou railway station, a hub in China’s high-speed rail network.
 Further research may make when, where and how the virus got into people clearer. There is scope for a lot more virus hunting in a wider range of possible intermediate species. If it were possible to conduct detailed interviews with those who came down with the earliest cases of covid-19, that genetic sampling could be better aimed, says Dr Embarek, and with a bit of luck one might get to the source. But the time needed to do this, he adds, “might be quick, or it might be extremely long”.
 If it turns out to have originated elsewhere, the new virus’s identification during the early stages of the Wuhan epidemic may turn out to be thanks to the city’s concentration of virological know-how—know-how that is now surely being thrown into sequencing more viruses from more sources. But until a satisfactory account of a natural spillover is achieved, that same concentration of know-how, at   and another local research centre, the Wuhan Centre for Disease Control and Prevention, will continue to attract suspicion.
  In 2017   opened the first biosecurity-level 4 ( -4) laboratory in China—the sort of high-containment facility in which work is done on the most dangerous pathogens. A large part of Dr Shi’s post-  research there has been aimed at understanding the potential which viruses still circulating among bats have to spill over into the human population. In one experiment she and Ge Xingyi, also of the  , in collaboration with American and Italian scientists, explored the disease-like potential of a bat coronavirus,  014- o , by recombining its genome with that of a mouse-infecting coronavirus. The   newsletter of November 2015 reported that the resulting virus could “replicate efficiently in primary human airway cells and achieve  in vitro titres equivalent to epidemic strains of  - o ”. In early April this newsletter and all others were removed from the institute’s website.
 This work, results from which were also published in  Nature Medicine, demonstrated that  - o ’s jump from bats to humans had not been a fluke; other bat coronaviruses were capable of something similar. Useful to know. But giving pathogens and potential pathogens extra powers in order to understand what they may be capable of is a controversial undertaking. These “gain of function” experiments, their proponents insist, have important uses such as understanding drug resistance and the tricks viruses employ to evade the immune system. They also carry obvious risks: the techniques on which they depend could be abused; their products could leak. The creation of an enhanced strain of bird flu in 2011 in an attempt to understand the peculiar virulence of the flu strain responsible for the pandemic of 1918-19 caused widespread alarm. America stopped funding gain-of-function work for several years.
  Filippa Lentzos, who studies biomedicine and security at King’s College, London, says the possibility of  - o -2 having an origin connected with legitimate research is being discussed widely in the world of biosecurity. The possibilities speculated about include a leak of material from a laboratory and also the accidental infection of a human being in the course of work either in a lab or in the field.
 Leaks from laboratories, including  -4 labs, are not unheard of. The world’s last known case of smallpox was caused by a leak from a British laboratory in 1978. An outbreak of foot and mouth disease in 2007 had a similar origin. In America there have been accidental releases and mishandlings involving Ebola, and, from a lower-containment-level laboratory, a deadly strain of bird flu. In China laboratory workers seem to have been infected with   and transmitted it to contacts outside on at least two occasions.
   Things doubtless leak out of labs working at lower biosafety levels, too. But how much they do so is unknown, in part because people worry about them less. And as in other parts of this story the unknown is a Petri dish in which speculation can grow. This may be part of the reason for interest in a lab at the Wuhan Centre for Disease Control and Prevention. A preprint published on ResearchGate, a website, by two Chinese scientists and subsequently removed suggested that work done there may have been cause for concern. This lab is reported to have housed animals—including, for one study, hundreds of bats from Hubei and Zhejiang provinces—and to have specialised in pathogen collection.
 Richard Pilch, who works on chemical and biological weapons non-proliferation at the Middlebury Institute of International Studies, in California, says that there is one feature of the new virus which might conceivably have arisen during “passaging experiments” in which pathogens are passed between hosts so as to study the evolution of their ability to spread. This is the “polybasic cleavage site”, which might enhance infectivity.  - o -2 has such a site on its spike protein. Its closest relatives among bat coronaviruses do not. But though such a cleavage site could have arisen through passaging there is no evidence that, in this case, it did. It could also have evolved in the normal way as the virus passed from host to host. Dr Holmes, meanwhile, has said that there is “no evidence that  - o -2...originated in a laboratory in Wuhan, China.” Though others have speculated about coincidences and possibilities, no one has been able, as yet, to undermine that statement.
 Many scientists think that with so many biologists actively hunting for bat viruses, and gain-of-function work becoming more common, the world is at increasing risk of a laboratory-derived pandemic at some point. “One of my biggest hopes out of this pandemic is that we address this issue—it really worries me,” says Dr Pilch. Today there are around 70  -4 sites in 30 countries. More such facilities are planned.
 Again, though, it is necessary to consider the unknown. Every year there are tens of thousands of fatal cases of respiratory disease around the world of which the cause is mysterious. Some of them may be the result of unrecognised zoonoses. The question of whether they really are, and how those threats may stack up, needs attention. That attention needs laboratories. It also needs a degree of open co-operation that America is now degrading with accusations and reductions in funding, and that China has taken steps to suppress at source. That suppression has done nothing to help the country; indeed, by supporting speculation, it may yet harm it.  ■

title: 冠状病毒起源的谜团正在揭开面纱。
title_en: The pieces of the puzzle of Covid-19’s origin are coming to light

note:
签到订阅世界商业科技与科学文化新闻极客体育健康辩论世界冠状病毒唐纳德·特朗普疫苗芯片索莫德维拉/盖蒂图片安东尼·福奇博士是唐纳德·特朗普总统的顾问，也是美国民间英雄，因为他在大流行危机期间坚定、冷静的领导能力。至少有一项民意调查显示，美国人更信任福奇。
比特朗普在冠状病毒大流行上的表现更好-布拉德·皮特(Brad Pitt)在电视上饰演的科学家寥寥无几。但就在去年，由福奇博士领导的国家过敏和传染病研究所资助了武汉病毒学研究所和其他机构的科学家进行蝙蝠冠状病毒的功能性研究。
2019年，在NIAID的支持下，美国国立卫生研究院(National Institutes Of Health)承诺在六年内投入370万美元用于研究，其中包括一些功能增益工作。在此之前，另一个耗资370万美元的为期5年的收集和研究蝙蝠冠状病毒的项目于2019年结束，使总金额达到740万美元。
许多科学家批评了功能研究的收获，这种研究涉及在实验室操纵病毒，以探索它们感染人类的潜力，因为它带来了从意外释放开始大流行的风险。
现在引起全球大流行的SARS-CoV-2病毒据信起源于蝙蝠。美国情报机构最初断言冠状病毒是自然发生的，但上个月承认，大流行可能源于武汉实验室的泄漏。(在这一点上，大多数科学家说，大流行病毒有可能是被设计或操纵的，但可能性不大。)。
福奇博士没有回应“新闻周刊”的置评请求。作为回应，NIH发表了一份声明，部分内容是：大多数新出现的人类病毒来自野生动物，这些病毒对美国和全球的公共健康和生物安全构成了重大威胁，2002-03年的SARS疫情和目前的冠状病毒大流行证明了这一点。科学研究表明，没有证据表明这种病毒是在实验室里制造出来的。
这些有争议的实验和武汉实验室怀疑引发大流行的更多信息始于2014年，涉及对蝙蝠冠状病毒的监测，预算为370万美元。该项目资助了武汉实验室的病毒学家施正丽和其他研究人员对野生蝙蝠冠状病毒进行调查和编目。这部分工程于2019年竣工。从那一年开始，该项目的主要内容包括额外的监测工作，但也包括功能增益研究，目的是了解蝙蝠冠状病毒如何变异来攻击人类。该项目由非营利性研究组织生态健康联盟(EcoHealth Alliance)运营，由疾病生态学专家彼得·达萨克(Peter Daszak)总统领导。据Politico报道，就在上周五，即4月24日，NIH取消了该项目。Daszak没有立即回复《新闻周刊》的置评请求。该项目建议书声明：我们将利用S蛋白序列数据、感染性克隆技术、体外和体内感染实验以及受体结合分析来验证S蛋白序列中的%发散阈值预测溢出潜力的假说。#34；我们将使用S蛋白序列数据、感染性克隆技术、体外和体内感染实验以及受体结合分析来验证S蛋白序列中的%分歧阈值预测溢出潜力的假设。
用外行人的话说，溢出潜力指的是病毒从动物跳到人类的能力，这要求病毒能够附着在人类细胞中的受体上。例如，SARS-CoV-2擅长与人类肺部和其他器官中的ACE2受体结合。
据罗格斯大学传染病专家理查德·埃布赖特介绍，该项目描述指的是利用基因工程技术增强蝙蝠冠状病毒感染人类细胞和实验动物能力的实验。在大流行之后，这是一个值得注意的细节。
与许多其他科学家一样，埃布赖特一直直言不讳地反对功能获得研究，因为它存在通过意外从实验室释放而造成大流行的风险。
福奇博士因其在20世纪90年代关于艾滋病毒/艾滋病危机的工作而闻名。他出生于布鲁克林，1966年以全班第一名的成绩毕业于康奈尔大学医学院(Cornell University Medical College)。自1984年以来担任NIAID负责人，自罗纳德·里根(Ronald Reagan)以来，他曾担任每一位美国总统的顾问。
十年前，在一场关于禽流感病毒获得功能研究的争议中，福奇博士在推动这项工作方面发挥了重要作用。他认为，这项研究值得冒这样的风险，因为它使科学家能够做好准备，比如调查可能的抗病毒药物，如果发生大流行，这些准备可能会很有用。
正在讨论的工作是一种功能获得性研究，涉及到将野生病毒通过活体动物传播，直到它们变异成可能构成大流行威胁的形式。科学家们用它来提取一种在人类之间传播很差的病毒，并使其成为一种高度传播性的病毒-这是大流行病毒的一个标志。这项工作是通过感染一系列雪貂来完成的，让病毒发生变异，直到一只没有被故意感染的雪貂感染了这种疾病。
这项工作带来了即使是经验丰富的研究人员也担心的风险。200多名科学家呼吁停止这项工作。他们说，问题在于，这增加了通过实验室事故发生大流行的可能性。
中国建议美国对其冠状病毒反应隐瞒一些事情，福奇博士为这项工作进行了辩护。福奇和两位合著者在2011年12月30日的“华盛顿邮报”上写道，确定这些病毒的分子阿喀琉斯之踵可以让科学家识别新的抗病毒药物靶点，这些靶点可以用来预防高危人群的感染或更好地治疗感染者。几十年的经验告诉我们，将通过生物医学研究获得的信息传播给合法的科学家和卫生官员，为制定适当的对策并最终保护公众健康提供了重要的基础。
然而，2014年，在奥巴马政府的压力下，美国国立卫生研究院(National Of Health Institutes Of Health)暂停了这项工作，暂停了21项研究。
三年后-2017年12月-NIH结束了暂停，NIAID项目的第二阶段开始，其中包括功能获得研究。美国国立卫生研究院建立了一个确定研究将如何进行的框架：科学家必须获得专家小组的批准，专家小组将决定风险是否合理。
这些审查确实进行了-但是秘密进行的，NIH为此招致了批评。2019年初，在《科学》杂志的一名记者发现NIH批准了两个使用功能增益方法的流感研究项目后，反对这种研究的科学家在《华盛顿邮报》的一篇社论中严厉抨击了NIH。
约翰·霍普金斯大学(Johns Hopkins University)的汤姆·英格尔斯比(Tom Inglesby)和哈佛大学(Harvard)的马克·利普西奇(Marc Lipsitch)写道，我们严重怀疑是否应该进行这些实验。由于商议是闭门进行的，我们中没有人有机会了解政府是如何做出这些决定的，也没有人有机会判断这一过程的严谨性和完整性。

Sign In   Subscribe                       World  Business  Tech & Science  Culture  Newsgeek  Sports  Health  The Debate  Vantage  Weather                    By                       World  Coronavirus  Donald Trump  Vaccine                 Chip Somodevilla/Getty Images Dr. Anthony Fauci is an adviser to President Donald Trump and something of an American folk hero for his steady, calm leadership during the pandemic crisis. At least one poll shows that Americans  trust Fauci more
than Trump on the coronavirus pandemic—and few scientists are portrayed on TV by Brad Pitt. But just last year, the National Institute for Allergy and Infectious Diseases, the organization led by Dr. Fauci, funded scientists at the Wuhan Institute of Virology and other institutions for work on gain-of-function research on bat coronaviruses.
 In 2019, with the backing of NIAID, the National Institutes of Health committed $3.7 million over six years for research that included some gain-of-function work. The program followed another $3.7 million, 5-year project for collecting and studying bat coronaviruses, which ended in 2019, bringing the total to $7.4 million.
 Many scientists have criticized gain of function research, which involves manipulating viruses in the lab to explore their potential for infecting humans, because it creates a risk of starting a pandemic from accidental release.
 SARS-CoV-2 , the virus now causing a global pandemic, is believed to have originated in bats. U.S. intelligence, after originally asserting that the coronavirus had occurred naturally, conceded last month that the pandemic may have originated in a leak from the Wuhan lab. (At this point most scientists say it's possible—but not likely—that the pandemic virus was engineered or manipulated.)
 Dr. Fauci did not respond to  Newsweek's requests for comment. NIH responded with a statement that said in part: "Most emerging human viruses come from wildlife, and these represent a significant threat to public health and biosecurity in the US and globally, as demonstrated by the SARS epidemic of 2002-03, and the current COVID-19 pandemic.... scientific research indicates that there is no evidence that suggests the virus was created in a laboratory."
  The Controversial Experiments and Wuhan Lab Suspected of Starting Pandemic Read more     began in 2014 and involved surveillance of bat coronaviruses, and had a budget of $3.7 million. The program funded Shi Zheng-Li, a virologist at the Wuhan lab, and other researchers to investigate and catalogue bat coronaviruses in the wild. This part of the project was completed in 2019.  of the project, beginning that year, included additional surveillance work but also gain-of-function research for the purpose of understanding how bat coronaviruses could mutate to attack humans. The project was run by EcoHealth Alliance, a non-profit research group, under the direction of President Peter Daszak, an expert on disease ecology. NIH canceled the project just this past Friday, April 24th,  Politico reported. Daszak did not immediately respond to  Newsweek requests for comment. The project proposal states: "We will use S protein sequence data, infectious clone technology, in vitro and in vivo infection experiments and analysis of receptor binding to test the hypothesis that % divergence thresholds in S protein sequences predict spillover potential."
 In layman's terms, "spillover potential" refers to the ability of a virus to jump from animals to humans, which requires that the virus be able to attach to receptors in the cells of humans. SARS-CoV-2, for instance, is adept at binding to the ACE2 receptor in human lungs and other organs.
 According to Richard Ebright, an infectious disease expert at Rutgers University, the project description refers to experiments that would enhance the ability of bat coronavirus to infect human cells and laboratory animals using techniques of genetic engineering. In the wake of the pandemic, that is a noteworthy detail.
 Ebright, along with many other scientists, has been a vocal opponent of gain-of-function research because of the risk it presents of creating a pandemic through accidental release from a lab.
 Dr. Fauci is renowned for his work on the HIV/AIDS crisis in the 1990s. Born in Brooklyn, he graduated first in his class from Cornell University Medical College in 1966. As head of NIAID since 1984, he has served as an adviser to every U.S. president since Ronald Reagan.
 A decade ago, during a controversy over gain-of-function research on bird-flu viruses, Dr. Fauci played an important role in promoting the work. He argued that the research was worth the risk it entailed because it enables scientists to make preparations, such as investigating possible anti-viral medications, that could be useful if and when a pandemic occurred.
 The work in question was a type of gain-of-function research that involved taking wild viruses and passing them through live animals until they mutate into a form that could pose a pandemic threat. Scientists used it to take a virus that was poorly transmitted among humans and make it into one that was highly transmissible—a hallmark of a pandemic virus. This work was done by infecting a series of ferrets, allowing the virus to mutate until a ferret that hadn't been deliberately infected contracted the disease.
 The work entailed risks that worried even seasoned researchers. More than 200 scientists called for the work to be halted. The problem, they said, is that it increased the likelihood that a pandemic would occur through a laboratory accident.
  China Suggests U.S. Is 'Hiding Something' About Its Coronavirus Response Read more    Dr. Fauci defended the work. "[D]etermining the molecular Achilles' heel of these viruses can allow scientists to identify novel antiviral drug targets that could be used to prevent infection in those at risk or to better treat those who become infected," wrote Fauci and two co-authors in the  Washington Post on December 30, 2011. "Decades of experience tells us that disseminating information gained through biomedical research to legitimate scientists and health officials provides a critical foundation for generating appropriate countermeasures and, ultimately, protecting the public health."
 Nevertheless, in 2014, under pressure from the Obama administration, the National of Institutes of Health instituted a moratorium on the work, suspending 21 studies.
 Three years later, though—in December 2017—the NIH ended the moratorium and the second phase of the NIAID project, which included the gain-of-function research, began. The NIH established a framework for determining how the research would go forward: scientists have to get approval from a panel of experts, who would decide whether the risks were justified.
 The reviews were indeed conducted—but in secret, for which the NIH has drawn criticism. In early 2019, after a reporter for  Science magazine discovered that the NIH had approved two influenza research projects that used gain of function methods, scientists who oppose this kind of research excoriated the NIH in an editorial in the  Washington Post.
 "We have serious doubts about whether these experiments should be conducted at all," wrote Tom Inglesby of Johns Hopkins University and Marc Lipsitch of Harvard. "[W]ith deliberations kept behind closed doors, none of us will have the opportunity to understand how the government arrived at these decisions or to judge the rigor and integrity of that process."

note:
5月1日是我在亚马逊网络服务公司担任副总裁和杰出工程师的最后一天，在经历了五年零五个月的令人满意的乐趣之后。我在亚马逊解雇告密者时沮丧地辞职，这些举报人大声说仓库员工害怕冠状病毒。
加上大型技术公司的工资和股份夹克，这可能会花费我100多万美元(税前)，更不用说我有过的最好的工作，与非常好的人一起工作。所以我很郁闷。
发生了什么·去年，技术方面的亚马逊人联合起来，成为亚马逊气候正义组织(AECJ)的员工，他们第一次引起世界关注的是一封公开信，信中宣传了一项股东决议，呼吁亚马逊在全球气候紧急情况下采取戏剧性行动，发挥领导作用。我是它的8702个签字人之一。
虽然这项决议获得了很多选票，但没有获得通过。四个月后，来自世界各地的3000名亚马逊技术工人参加了全球气候罢工。罢工前一天，亚马逊宣布了一项大规模计划，旨在使该公司成为气候危机解决方案的一部分。这并不是说这些积极分子被他们的雇主承认有前瞻性；事实上，领导人受到了解雇的威胁。
快进到冠状病毒时代。亚马逊仓库发生骚乱的故事浮出水面，工人们对自己不知情、没有保护和恐惧发出警报。官方声明声称正在采取一切可能的安全预防措施。然后，一名组织改善安全条件的工人被解雇，泄露的高管会议记录中出现了残酷的麻木不仁的言论，会议重点是为亚马逊的“谈话要点”辩护。
仓库工人向AECJ寻求支持。作为回应，他们在内部发起了一项请愿活动，并在4月16日(星期四)组织了一场视频通话，来自世界各地的仓库工人参加了活动，嘉宾活动家内奥米·克莱因(Naomi Klein)也参加了。4月10日星期五发给内部邮件名单的公告显然是引爆点。艾米丽·坎宁安(Emily Cunningham)和马伦·科斯塔(Maren Costa)，两位可见的AECJ领导人，当天被当场解雇。这些理由是可笑的；任何理性的观察家都清楚地知道，他们是被揭发的。
管理层可以反对这一事件，或者要求将外部人士排除在外，或者要求代表领导层，或者其他任何数量的事情；有足够的时间。相反，他们只是解雇了这些积极分子。
砰！·在这一点上，我抓狂了。副总裁不应该在公共场合流氓，所以我通过适当的渠道和按规定升级了。我不能随意透露这些讨论，但我提出了这篇文章中出现的许多论点。我想我把它们送给了合适的人。
如此一来，继续担任亚马逊副总裁实际上意味着签署了我所鄙视的行动。所以我辞职了。
受害者不是抽象的实体，而是真实的人；以下是他们的一些名字：考特尼·鲍登、杰拉尔德·布莱森、马伦·科斯塔、艾米丽·坎宁安、巴希尔·穆罕默德和克里斯·斯莫尔斯。
我敢肯定他们每个人都是有色人种，女人，或者两者兼而有之，这肯定是巧合。对吗？
让我们让这些名字中的一个发声吧。巴希尔·穆罕默德(Bashir Mohamed)说，“他们解雇我是为了让其他人感到害怕。”你不同意吗？
“就像在你的额头上画一个牌子，上面写着‘要么有罪，要么有什么要隐瞒。’”
仓库呢？·事实上，工人们说他们在仓库里有危险。我不认为媒体在讲述他们的故事方面做得非常好。我去看了让马伦和艾米丽被解雇的视频聊天，发现他们听得很动人。如果你愿意，你也可以听。YouTube上有另一个全天的录像带，它有9个小时长，但有一个目录，你可以决定是想听到来自波兰、德国、法国还是美国多个地方的人的声音。“纽约时报”有更多报道。
不只是工人们心烦意乱。以下是来自14个州的总检察长发表的言论。以下是纽约州总检察长的详细投诉。这是亚马逊在法国法庭上两次败诉。
另一方面，亚马逊的信息一直很紧迫，他们正在优先考虑这个问题，并在仓库安全方面投入大量努力。我实际上相信这一点：我从我信任的人那里听到了关于紧张的工作和巨大的投资的详细描述。这对他们有好处；让我们承认，你不会在很短的时间内就把一艘超级油轮翻个底朝天。
但我也相信工人的证词。归根结底，最大的问题不是冠状病毒反应的具体情况。这是因为亚马逊将仓库中的人类视为具有拣选和打包潜力的可替换单位。不过，这不仅仅是亚马逊，21世纪资本主义就是这么做的。
亚马逊管理得非常好，在发现机会和构建可重复的利用这些机会的流程方面表现出了高超的技能。相应地，它对财富和权力的持续增长和积累带来的人类成本缺乏远见。如果我们不喜欢亚马逊正在做的某些事情，我们需要设置法律护栏来阻止这些事情。我们不需要发明任何新东西；严格执行的反垄断、生活工资和工人赋权立法的结合，提供了一条明确的前进道路。
不要说做不到，因为法国正在做。
毒药·解雇举报人不仅是宏观经济力量的副作用，也不是自由市场功能的内在原因。这证明了公司文化中流淌着一种有毒的血脉。我选择既不上也不喝那毒药。
AWS呢？·亚马逊网络服务(该公司的“云计算”部门)，我工作的地方，则是另一回事。它以人道的态度对待员工，努力实现工作和生活的平衡，努力推动多元化(大多数情况下都失败了，但其他所有人都是如此)，总的来说，它是一个有道德的组织。我由衷地钦佩它的领导力。
当然，它的工人有权力。平均工资很高，任何不开心的人都可以走到街对面，找到另一份薪水相同或更高的工作。
找出一种模式？·归根结底，这一切都是关于权力平衡的。仓库工人很虚弱，而且越来越虚弱，原因是大规模失业和(在美国)与工作相关的医疗保险。所以他们会被当作垃圾对待，因为资本主义。任何看似合理的解决方案都必须从增强他们的集体力量开始。
下一步是什么？·给我的？我不知道，真的没有花时间去考虑。我很难过，但我现在呼吸更自由了。
看到你离开我很难过。感谢您在过去几年中所给予的帮助！一切顺利！
感谢你们为正义挺身而出。气候变化和工人权利是相关的。如果人类想要生存下去，问题不能再等了，谢谢你所做的，向权力说出真相。
note_en:
May 1 st was my last day as a VP and Distinguished Engineer at Amazon Web Services, after five years and five months of rewarding fun. I quit in dismay at Amazon firing whistleblowers who were making noise about warehouse employees frightened of Covid-19.
 What with big-tech salaries and share vestings, this will probably cost me over a million (pre-tax) dollars, not to mention the best job I’ve ever had, working with awfully good people. So I’m pretty blue.
 What happened · Last year, Amazonians on the tech side banded together as  Amazon Employees for Climate Justice (AECJ), first coming to the world’s notice with an  open letter promoting a shareholders’ resolution calling for dramatic action and leadership from Amazon on the global climate emergency. I was one of its 8,702 signatories.
 While the resolution got a lot of votes, it didn’t pass. Four months later, 3,000 Amazon tech workers from around the world  joined in the Global Climate Strike walkout. The day before the walkout, Amazon announced  a large-scale plan aimed at making the company part of the climate-crisis solution. It’s not as though the activists were acknowledged by their employer for being forward-thinking; in fact, leaders were  threatened with dismissal.
 Fast-forward to the Covid-19 era. Stories surfaced of unrest in Amazon warehouses, workers raising alarms about being uninformed, unprotected, and frightened. Official statements claimed every possible safety precaution was being taken. Then a worker organizing for better safety conditions was fired, and  brutally insensitive remarks appeared in leaked executive meeting notes where the focus was on defending Amazon “talking points”.
 Warehouse workers reached out to AECJ for support. They responded by internally promoting  a petition and organizing a video call for Thursday April 16 featuring warehouse workers from around the world, with guest activist  Naomi Klein. An announcement sent to internal mailing lists on Friday April 10th was apparently the flashpoint. Emily Cunningham and Maren Costa, two visible AECJ leaders, were fired on the spot that day. The justifications were laughable; it was clear to any reasonable observer that they were turfed for whistleblowing.
 Management could have objected to the event, or demanded that outsiders be excluded, or that leadership be represented, or any number of other things; there was plenty of time. Instead, they just fired the activists.
 Snap! · At that point I snapped. VPs shouldn’t go publicly rogue, so I escalated through the proper channels and by the book. I’m not at liberty to disclose those discussions, but I made many of the arguments appearing in this essay. I think I made them to the appropriate people.
 That done, remaining an Amazon VP would have meant, in effect, signing off on actions I despised. So I resigned.
 The victims weren’t abstract entities but real people; here are some of their names: Courtney Bowden, Gerald Bryson, Maren Costa, Emily Cunningham, Bashir Mohammed, and Chris Smalls.
 I’m sure it’s a coincidence that every one of them is a person of color, a woman, or both. Right?
 Let’s give one of those names a voice.  Bashir Mohamed said “They fired me to make others scared.” Do you disagree?
  “Like painting a sign on your forehead saying ‘Either guilty, or has something to hide.’”
  What about the warehouses? · It’s a matter of fact that workers are saying they’re at risk in the warehouses. I don’t think the media’s done a terribly good job of telling their stories. I went to the video chat that got Maren and Emily fired, and found listening to them moving. You can listen too if you’d like.  Up on YouTube is another full-day videochat; it’s nine hours long, but there’s a table of contents, you can decide whether you want to hear people from Poland, Germany, France, or multiple places in the USA. Here’s  more reportage from the NY Times.
 It’s not just workers who are upset. Here are  Attorneys-general from 14 states speaking out. Here’s the  New York State Attorney-general with more detailed complaints. Here’s  Amazon losing in French courts, twice.
 On the other hand, Amazon’s messaging has been urgent that they are prioritizing this issue and putting massive efforts into warehouse safety. I actually believe this: I have heard detailed descriptions from people I trust of the intense work and huge investments. Good for them; and let’s grant that you don’t turn a supertanker on a dime.
 But I believe the worker testimony too. And at the end of the day, the big problem isn’t the specifics of Covid-19 response. It’s that Amazon treats the humans in the warehouses as fungible units of pick-and-pack potential. Only that’s not just Amazon, it’s how 21st-century capitalism is done.
 Amazon is exceptionally well-managed and has demonstrated great skill at spotting opportunities and building repeatable processes for exploiting them. It has a corresponding lack of vision about the human costs of the relentless growth and accumulation of wealth and power. If we don’t like certain things Amazon is doing, we need to put legal guardrails in place to stop those things. We don’t need to invent anything new; a combination of antitrust and living-wage and worker-empowerment legislation, rigorously enforced, offers a clear path forward.
 Don’t say it can’t be done, because  France is doing it.
 Poison · Firing whistleblowers isn’t just a side-effect of macroeconomic forces, nor is it intrinsic to the function of free markets. It’s evidence of a vein of toxicity running through the company culture. I choose neither to serve nor drink that poison.
 What about AWS? · Amazon Web Services (the “Cloud Computing” arm of the company), where I worked, is a different story. It treats its workers humanely, strives for work/life balance, struggles to move the diversity needle (and mostly fails, but so does everyone else), and is by and large an ethical organization. I genuinely admire its leadership.
 Of course, its workers have power. The average pay is very high, and anyone who’s unhappy can walk across the street and get another job paying the same or better.
 Spot a pattern? · At the end of the day, it’s all about power balances. The warehouse workers are weak and getting weaker, what with mass unemployment and (in the US) job-linked health insurance. So they’re gonna get treated like crap, because capitalism. Any plausible solution has to start with increasing their collective strength.
 What’s next? · For me? I don’t know, genuinely haven’t taken time to think about it. I’m sad, but I’m breathing more freely.
      Sad to see you leave. Thank you for the helps through the last few years! All the best!
 Thank you for standing up for justice. Climate Change and workers rights are related. And the problems can’t wait any longer if humanity is to survive, Thank you for doing what you did, to speak truth to power.

note:
汉龙的剃须刀是一句经典格言，我相信你以前听过：不要把可以用愚蠢来充分解释的事情归因于恶意。
我发现，当你不明白事情为什么会以某种方式出现时，最常见的原因既不是恶意，也不是愚蠢。相反，根本原因可能只是他们还没有时间。这种情况在初创企业中经常发生(大公司可能会少一些，原因我稍后再谈)。
我不明白为什么X团队不做功能创意Y。这是如此明显的事情要做！
为什么漏洞Z仍然存在？这件事不是很久以前就知道了吗？我不明白他们为什么不修理它？
我不明白为什么人力资源部仍然不提供福利W，其他很多公司都有！
我已经告诉我的经理，我们需要一个流程来处理第五件事，但它仍然没有发生！
当然，这些事情没有发生的原因是其他事情更重要。引用维基百科关于机会成本的话：
当从备选方案中选择一个选项时，机会成本是由于没有享受到与最佳备选方案相关的好处而产生的“成本”。“新牛津美语词典”将其定义为“当选择一个备选方案时，从其他备选方案中获得的潜在收益的损失。”简单地说，机会成本就是由于没有选择下一个最佳方案而没有获得的收益。
因此，我开始告诉人们：机会成本可以充分解释的事情，千万不要归因于愚蠢。
按照我的说法，机会成本是罪魁祸首，这似乎是显而易见的。但事实并非如此。人类心理学以一种奇怪的方式工作。人们喜欢总结说，有些事情没有做是因为他们愚蠢，或者可能是因为他们懒惰。
当你不认识某个人/团队时，大约95%的时间都会发生这种情况。在CI/CD系统上工作的团队只是一个在不同办公室工作的无名小卒，他们肯定是完全愚蠢，从来没有修过界面上超级烦人的按钮。他们整天在做什么！当你认识某个团队/人时，这个百分比会下降一点：也许只有40%的人会首先想到愚蠢。我显然是在编造这些数字，但你明白我的意思。
我在卫星办公室工作了很多年。很多时候，当我对某件事有严重的技术分歧时，我会飞到主要总部，和另一个团队出去吃饭。我甚至不会谈论技术，只会谈论随机的东西。一旦他们了解我，并意识到我是一个人(而不是一个无名小卒)，大多数技术上的分歧往往会消失。人们更可能采取积极的意图，而不是恶意/愚蠢/懒惰。
错误地假设机会成本是原因，这就排除了任何邪恶/愚蠢/懒惰的叙述：团队/人可能实际上只是有其他更重要的优先事项。但是他们是怎么挑选的呢？
我不会就如何评估你的JIRA董事会的努力和影响提供任何聪明的建议。有一万亿种方法、工具和流程可以做到这一点。但我逐渐相信的是：在任何一家公司，优先排序都是最能创造价值的活动。产生想法和执行事情当然也很重要！但是，我所看到的区分优秀团队和优秀团队的是对优先顺序的残酷关注。这意味着产生大量荒谬的想法，并将99%的想法抛诸脑后，专注于影响最大的1%。
创意的产生速度会比执行它们的带宽快得多，所以如果你的积压工作无限期地增长，你就是在做正确的事情。在一个平庸的团队中，一个消极的人会抱怨说，从来没有时间去做他们最喜欢的项目X。我经常听到这样的话：“我们积压的功能一直在快速增长，我们怎么会有时间投资偿还科技债务呢？”
对我来说，这反映了对产品开发应该如何工作的误解。积压的订单应该会无限期地增长。一个好的团队要做的就是接受这一点，在产品和技术之间建立良好的关系，并确保你不断地重新安排优先顺序。也许今天，它将提供业务所需的一系列功能。也许明天它将偿还一些科技债务。如果你有一个共同的框架来思考价值和优先顺序，它通常是可行的。
我顺便提到，机会成本可能是初创公司的一个原因，但在大公司可能不太常见。为什么？因为一家初创公司经常在追赶，建立一些大多是“显而易见”的东西。当你到了一个很晚的阶段，你有很多钱和很多开发人员，事情就会变得复杂得多。你不能做X，因为它会蚕食Y指标，让广告商不高兴。你不能做Z，因为那会与Q的工作方式不一致。诸若此类。
我们从一个看似无关紧要的声明开始，但它很快就导致了许多推论：如何信任人，如何管理积压的工作，如何确定优先顺序如何产生巨大的价值。有趣的!。
我经常不断地向人们引用转述的引语，但人们并不知道我在说什么。希望今后，有这篇博客帖子可供人们参考！

note_en:
Hanlon's razor is a classic aphorism I'm sure you have heard before:  Never attribute to malice that which can be adequately explained by stupidity.
 I've found that neither malice nor stupidity is the most common reason when you don't understand why something is in a certain way. Instead, the root cause is probably just that  they didn't have time yet. This happens all the time at startups (maybe a bit less at big companies, for reasons I'll get back to).
  I don't understand why  team X isn't working on  feature idea Y. It's such an obvious thing to do!
 Why is  bug Z still present? Hasn't it been known for a really long time? I don't understand why they aren't fixing it?
 I don't get why HR team still doesn't offer  perk W. So many other companies have that!
 I've told my manager that we need a process for  thing V but it still hasn't happened!
 Of course, why these things never happened is that  something else was more important. Quoting Wikipedia on  Opportunity cost:
 When an option is chosen from alternatives, the opportunity cost is the “cost” incurred by not enjoying the benefit associated with the best alternative choice. The New Oxford American Dictionary defines it as “the loss of potential gain from other alternatives when one alternative is chosen.” In simple terms, opportunity cost is the benefit not received as a result of not selecting the next best option.
 Thus I've started telling people:  Never attribute to stupidity that which is adequately explained by opportunity cost.
  It might seem obvious the way I put it that opportunity cost is the thing to blame. But it's not. Human psychology works in weird ways. People love to conclude that something wasn't done because they are  stupid, or possibly lazy.
 This happens about 95% of the time when you don't  know a certain person/team. The team that works on the CI/CD system is just a faceless blob in a different office and they must be completely stupid for never fixing the super annoying button in the interface. What are they doing all day! When you  know a certain team/person, the percentage drops a bit: maybe stupidity is the first thought only 40% of the time. I'm obviously just making up these numbers, but you get the point.
  I spent many years working in a satellite office. A lot of the time when I had some deep technical disagreement about something, I'd fly to the main HQ, and go out for dinner with the other team. I wouldn't even talk about technology, just about random stuff. Once they knew me, and realize I was a human (and not a faceless blob), most technical disagreements tended to go away. People are more likely to assume positive intent and not malice/stupidity/laziness.
  Erring on the side of assuming opportunity cost as cause precludes any evil/stupid/lazy narrative: the team/person probably actually just had other more important priorities. But how did they pick those?
 I'm not going to offer any smart advice on how to estimate effort and impact in your JIRA board. There's a trillion methods and tools and processes for doing that. But what I have come to believe is that: prioritization is the most value creating activity in any company. Generating ideas and executing things is of course also important! But what I've seen to set apart great teams from good is a brutal focus on  prioritization. This means generating an absurd amount of ideas and throwing 99% of them out of the window, to focus on the 1% that have the highest impact.
  Ideas will be generated much faster than there's bandwidth to execute on them, so you're doing something  right if your backlog is growing indefinitely. A negative person on a mediocre team will complain that there's never time to work on  their favorite pet project X. I've often heard things like “our backlog of features keeps growing so fast, how are we ever going to have time to invest in paying down tech debt?".
 To me this reflects a misunderstanding of how product development  should work. Backlogs  should be growing indefinitely. What a  good team will do is to accept that, and establish a good relationship between product and tech, and make sure you constantly keep reprioritizing. Maybe today it's shipping a bunch of features the business needs. Maybe tomorrow it's paying down some tech debt. If you have a shared framework for how to think about value and prioritization, it usually works out.
  I mentioned in passing that opportunity cost is a likely cause at startups, but maybe less often at big companies. Why? Because a startup is often playing catch up building things that are mostly “obvious”. When you get to a very late stage, and you have a lot of money and a lot of developers, things get a lot more tricky. You can't do X because it will cannibalize metric Y and upset advertisers. You can't do Z because that would be inconsistent with how Q works. And so on.
   We started with one seemingly innocuous statement, but it quickly led to many corollaries: how to trust people, how to manage the backlog, how prioritization can generate tremendous value. Fun!
 I often keep quoting the paraphrased quote to people, but people have no clue what I'm talking about. Hopefully going forward, there's this blog post to refer people to!
   Tagged with:  management
title: 不要把机会成本能充分解释的事情归因于愚蠢。
title_en: Never attribute to stupidity what is adequately explained by opportunity cost
